column1: All the failing tests point to a handful of common root causes:

column2: Two login‑flows create two separate Session objects. The second one overwrites the first in the global cache, so the user‑agent from the first flow is lost – a “pollution” bug.
column3: Keep a per‑user (or per‑agent) mapping, or clear the cache between flows.

column2: The route calls ConversationService.get_by_id() but the service returns a 404‑style error instead of the expected Conversation object.
column3: The service should return the object or raise a ConversationError only when the ID does not exist.

column2: The workflow crashes because the chat_completion function is not registered or is missing a required parameter.
column3: Register the function correctly in the workflow and ensure the signature matches the call.

column1: All ConversationService tests (create, get, list, delete)
• Returning an empty list from list.
• Receiving the wrong keyword argument (db_session vs session).
column2: Update the constructor to accept the right keyword (session), persist the conversation, and return the proper objects.
Same as above – the service cannot find the conversation.
The UserSettingsService.update() method fails validation when the setting value already exists.
column3: Relax the validation or allow overwrite.

column2: The returned list does not contain the setting_key field, causing the assertion to fail.
column3: Ensure the schema used for serialization includes setting_key.
